skill.name: verification-before-completion
skill.description: Requires concrete verification before an assistant claims work is done, fixed, or working. It guides agents to run relevant checks, show output, and explain any skipped verification.
skill.summary: Add a final verification checkpoint before completion claims.
skill.author: ANcpLua

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks and one weak-cryptography pattern, but the weak-cryptography finding and most backtick hits are false positives. The skill does direct agents to run build, test, and dev commands, which is legitimate for verification but requires normal command-safety review before execution.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Directed External Command Use
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run commands such as dotnet build, dotnet test, and npm run dev before claiming completion. This is not executable malware, but agents must verify commands are appropriate for the current project before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and repeated, so the external-command risk is real. Confidence is moderate because the commands are normal verification commands in Markdown guidance, not hidden executable code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: Several static Ruby shell backtick findings are Markdown code fences or quoted examples. They describe output formats and examples, and no evidence found shows Ruby code or automatic shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visibly Markdown fenced blocks or example text. There is no Ruby file, interpreter directive, or executable wrapper in the scanned skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography finding points to the frontmatter description block. No evidence found shows a cryptographic algorithm, hash operation, or credential handling in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is YAML frontmatter for the human-readable description. The reviewed file contains no cryptographic code or crypto-related instructions.

content.value_statement: AI assistants can claim work is complete before checking the result. This skill adds a final checkpoint that requires tests, build output, sources, or a clear reason for skipped verification.
actual_capabilities: Prompts an assistant to verify work before saying it is done.
Provides checklists for code changes, factual claims, bug fixes, and working-state claims.
Requires build, test, execution, or source evidence when relevant.
Encourages clear reporting of skipped checks and remaining uncertainty.
Shows a structured verification summary format for final responses.
limitations: It does not run tests or commands by itself.
It cannot guarantee that available tests cover every defect.
It depends on the assistant choosing checks that match the project.
It may add extra time to small or low-risk tasks.

use_cases.title: Confirm Code Changes
use_cases.description: Require build and test evidence before an assistant says an implementation is complete.

use_cases.title: Validate Bug Fixes
use_cases.description: Guide the assistant to reproduce the issue, apply the fix, and verify the failure no longer occurs.

use_cases.title: Check Time-Sensitive Claims
use_cases.description: Push the assistant to verify dates, versions, and product status before making factual claims.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Completion Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Use after a small code or documentation task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Before you say this is complete, apply the verification-before-completion checklist and tell me what you checked.

prompt_templates.title: Bug Fix Verification
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when asking an assistant to fix a reported defect.
prompt_templates.prompt: After making the fix, show how you reproduced the bug, what changed, and what verification proves the bug is resolved.

prompt_templates.title: Release Readiness Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use before merging or publishing a change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the relevant verification steps for this repository and summarize build status, test status, skipped checks, and remaining risk.

prompt_templates.title: Evidence-Gated Completion
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for high-risk work where unsupported claims are unacceptable.
prompt_templates.prompt: Do not claim success until you provide direct evidence from commands, source checks, or citations. If verification is incomplete, state exactly what is missing.

output_examples.input: The assistant has finished a code change and is ready to report completion.
output_examples.output: A concise verification summary that lists build status, test status, steps performed, confidence level, and any checks that were skipped.

output_examples.input: The assistant fixed a bug but has not shown evidence.
output_examples.output: A response that pauses the completion claim, explains the missing verification, and requests or performs the needed reproduction and regression checks.

output_examples.input: The assistant makes a claim about a current framework version.
output_examples.output: A response that verifies the version against a current source before presenting the claim with a citation.

best_practices: Match verification steps to the project language, framework, and risk level.
Show actual command results or cite sources before claiming completion.
State skipped checks plainly, with the reason and remaining risk.
anti_patterns: Claiming a fix works without running the affected path.
Treating a successful build as proof that behavior is correct.
Using this skill to run unrelated or unsafe commands.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides verification guidance for the assistant to follow in the active project context.

faq.question: Is it only for .NET projects?
faq.answer: No. The examples mention .NET, but the verification pattern applies to any stack.

faq.question: Can it replace a test suite?
faq.answer: No. It helps ensure tests or other checks are run, but it does not create coverage by itself.

faq.question: How should skipped tests be handled?
faq.answer: The assistant should state the reason, the impact, and what would be needed to complete verification.

faq.question: Does it help with factual research?
faq.answer: Yes. It reminds the assistant to verify dates, versions, and status claims with current sources.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: The skill encourages command execution, so each command should be reviewed for relevance and safety before running.
